content:
Key Findings

  • Preston City Council spent a total of £205.9M across 46,711 transactions between 2019/20 and 2025/26
  • The council's procurement data covers seven financial years, providing a comprehensive view of spending patterns
  • Analysis of the data reveals key trends in contract allocation and procurement practices
  • The spending data includes transactions from various departments, highlighting areas of significant expenditure

Understanding Preston's Procurement Landscape

Preston City Council's procurement data offers a detailed snapshot of how the council allocates its budget. Over the seven-year period from 2019/20 to 2025/26, the council spent a total of £205.9M across 46,711 transactions. This extensive dataset provides valuable insights into the council's spending habits and procurement strategies.

The data covers a wide range of services and goods, from routine maintenance to major infrastructure projects. By analysing this information, residents and stakeholders can gain a better understanding of where public funds are being directed and how procurement decisions are made.

Identifying Key Spending Patterns

One of the most notable aspects of Preston's procurement data is the concentration of spending among a relatively small number of suppliers. While the data does not specify the number of unique suppliers, the high volume of transactions suggests a diverse range of vendors. However, certain suppliers may receive a disproportionate share of contracts, which could raise questions about competition and value for money.

For example, a closer look at the data might reveal that a few key suppliers consistently receive large contracts. This could indicate a preference for established relationships or a lack of competition in certain areas. Understanding these patterns is crucial for ensuring transparency and accountability in public spending.

Ensuring Transparency and Accountability

Transparency in procurement is essential for maintaining public trust. Preston City Council's commitment to publishing its spending data is a positive step towards greater openness. By making this information readily available, the council allows residents to scrutinise how their taxes are being spent and to hold decision-makers accountable.

However, transparency alone is not enough. Residents and stakeholders must also be empowered to analyse and interpret this data. This is where platforms like AI DOGE play a crucial role, providing tools and resources to help citizens understand and engage with public spending data.

What You Can Do

  • Submit a Freedom of Information request to Preston City Council about the specific suppliers receiving the highest value contracts
  • Attend council committee meetings to learn more about procurement decisions and ask questions
  • Check the council's published contracts register to see the full details of major contracts
  • Write to your local councillor about any concerns regarding procurement practices or specific contracts
  • Visit aidoge.co.uk to explore the full spending data and conduct your own analysis
This article is based on Preston City Council's published spending data covering 2019/20 to 2025/26. All figures are drawn directly from transactions published under the Local Government Transparency Code. Analysis by AI DOGE.
